title: Anterior Uveitis / Iritis
oneLiner: Acute inflammation of the iris and ciliary body causing painful red eye, photophobia, and blurred vision; common, treatable, but blind if missed.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of a red, painful left eye. She says the pain is a dull ache that worsens when she moves her eye or looks at bright lights; she keeps the curtains drawn. She reports blurred vision in that eye and a small headache. On exam, her visual acuity is 6/9 (20/30) in the affected eye. There is a ring of redness around the cornea (ciliary flush) and the left pupil is smaller than the right and slightly irregular. She winces when you shine a light (photophobia). You perform a slit lamp exam and see cells and flare in the anterior chamber.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Unilateral red eye | Rules out most bilateral causes (conjunctivitis, dry eye) — suggests intraocular inflammation |
| Pain on eye movement | Ciliary spasm from inflammation; not seen in simple conjunctivitis |
| Photophobia (bright lights → pain) | Miosis and iris spasm; hallmark of anterior uveitis |
| Blurred vision | Inflammatory cells and protein in the aqueous humor scatter light; also possible corneal edema |
| Ciliary flush (circumcorneal injection) | Deep injection around the limbus — indicates pathology in the anterior chamber or cornea |
| Small, irregular pupil | Posterior synechiae: the iris sticks to the lens — a *pathognomonic* sign of iritis |
| Cells and flare on slit lamp | The *sine qua non* — white cells (cells) and protein (flare) in the aqueous humor |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Bilateral, chronic, insidious** – patient may have no pain, just mild blurring. Think *sarcoidosis* or *sympathetic ophthalmia* (rare).
- **Granulomatous iritis** – you see large, “mutton-fat” keratic precipitates on the corneal endothelium and iris nodules. Etiology: sarcoid, TB, syphilis.
- **Pediatric iritis** – child with a white, painless red eye; no photophobia. Often associated with *juvenile idiopathic arthritis* (ANA+). Can be missed until band keratopathy or cataract develops.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Anterior uveitis (iritis) is inflammation of the iris and the ciliary body — the front part of the uveal tract. That inflammation leaks white blood cells and protein into the front of the eye (anterior chamber), causing a red, painful, light-sensitive eye with blurred vision.

**Why it matters:**

- **Frequency:** It is the most common form of uveitis (about 50% of all uveitis cases). You will see it on call.
- **Stakes:** Untreated, it leads to posterior synechiae → iris bombe → acute angle-closure glaucoma (blindness). Also, cataract, chronic glaucoma, and macular edema are possible.
- **Common pitfall:** Mistaking it for simple conjunctivitis and not doing a slit lamp exam. Antibiotic drops won’t help; topical steroids can cure it — if used correctly. Miss the diagnosis, and vision is lost.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
The disease is a story of **disrupted blood–aqueous barrier** and **immune activation**.

1. **Trigger** (infectious, autoimmune, trauma, idiopathic) → activation of Toll-like receptors and release of cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) → vasodilation and increased vascular permeability in the iris and ciliary body.
2. **Protein leaks** from blood into the anterior chamber → **flare** (visible on slit lamp as a beam of light in the aqueous humor).
3. **White blood cells** (neutrophils, lymphocytes, plasma cells) enter the aqueous humor → **cells**.
4. **Prostaglandins** (from COX-2 pathway) cause *ciliary muscle spasm* → **pain on accommodation (reading)** and **deep ache**.
5. **Iris sphincter spasm** (also prostaglandin-mediated) → **miosis**. This is why the pupil is small. The spasm causes **photophobia** (light tries to make the pupil smaller, hitting an already spastic muscle → pain).
6. **Fibrin exudate** forms from the leak → if the iris touches the lens, fibrin can glue them together → **posterior synechiae**. That's the irregular pupil — when you dilate it, the adhesions pull the pupil into a “daisy” shape.
7. If synechiae are 360°, aqueous humor cannot flow from behind the iris into the anterior chamber → **iris bombe** → the iris bulges forward → blocks the trabecular meshwork → **acute angle-closure glaucoma**.

**Link to pattern recognition:**  
- Unilateral → because a local trigger (e.g., HSV reactivation) or a systemic disease (HLA-B27) often affects one eye first.
- Ciliary flush → deep injection from dilated ciliary vessels (not superficial conjunctival vessels).
- Cells/flare → direct evidence of breakdown of blood–aqueous barrier.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | No cause found – 50% of cases | Young adult, no systemic symptoms, negative workup |
| **HLA-B27 related** | Ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis (Reiter’s), psoriatic arthritis, IBD | Unilateral, recurrent; ask about back pain, heel pain, urethritis, diarrhea, skin rash, nail pits. Pain is classic: *“wakes me up at night, better with movement”* |
| **Infectious** | HSV-1 (most common viral cause) | Corneal dendrites on fluorescein; decreased corneal sensation |
| | VZV (shingles) | Previous dermatomal rash; Hutchinson’s sign (vesicles on nose tip – nasociliary nerve) |
| | Toxoplasmosis (congenital or acquired) | Usually posterior uveitis, but can cause anterior in immunocompromised |
| | Syphilis (secondary or tertiary) | Rash, chancre, lymphadenopathy; often bilateral granulomatous |
| | Tuberculosis | Granulomatous; chest X-ray, IGRA positive; exposure history |
| | CMV (in immunocompromised) | Very high IOP, corneal endotheliitis; in HIV with CD4 < 100 |
| **Systemic autoimmune** | Sarcoidosis | Bilateral, granulomatous, mutton-fat KP; CXR shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy; elevated ACE |
| | Behçet disease | Oral ulcers, genital ulcers, skin lesions, arthritis; hypopyon is classic (visible layer of pus in anterior chamber) |
| | Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) | Young girl (5-10 years), ANA-positive, asymptomatic eye – no pain, no redness until late. Screening slit lambs every 3-6 months |
| **Trauma** | Blunt or penetrating injury | History of trauma; high suspicion for infectious endophthalmitis if penetrating |
| **Post-surgical** | After cataract surgery (toxic lens syndrome or pseudophakic uveitis) | Recent eye surgery, onset within days to weeks; can be infectious (endophthalmitis) or sterile inflammation |

**Most common cause overall: Idiopathic (~50%).** In patients with a systemic association, **HLA-B27-related** is the most common identifiable cause.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** None formal — a clinical diagnosis made by **slit lamp exam** showing cells and flare in the anterior chamber.

**Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Slit lamp exam** | Cells (0–4 grade), flare, keratic precipitates, synechiae | Diagnostic; also grades severity | Cells ≥ 1+ = diagnostic. Flare alone can be from low protein (like in quiet eye). |
| **Intraocular pressure (IOP)** | Can be low (due to decreased aqueous secretion) or high (due to trabecular inflammation or synechiae) | Baseline and to monitor for glaucoma | IOP < 10 = may be due to ciliary shutdown; IOP > 21 = suspect inflammatory glaucoma |
| **Dilated fundus exam** | Check for posterior synechiae, vitreous cells, disc edema | To rule out posterior involvement (panuveitis) and to break synechiae early | Dilating preps are essential |
| **Blood tests** (ESR, CRP) | Elevated in systemic inflammation | Screen for underlying disease | Non-specific; done if recurrent, bilateral, or granulomatous |
| **HLA-B27** | Positive in ~50% of acute anterior uveitis | To identify spondyloarthropathy | If positive, ask about back/joint symptoms even if absent on history |
| **Syphilis serology** (VDRL/TPPA) | Positive in syphilis | Mandatory in all uveitis (can mimic anything) | Even if no rash/chancre, treat if positive |
| **Quantiferon-TB (IGRA)** | Positive in latent TB infection | Consider in granulomatous or refractory cases | Start TB treatment if positive (after ruling out active TB) |
| **ANA** | Positive in JIA | In pediatric patients or young women with chronic bilateral disease | If positive, refer to rheumatology |
| **Chest X-ray** | Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (sarcoid), TB, other | Screen for sarcoid and TB | If bilateral hilar adenopathy on CXR and uveitis = sarcoid until proven otherwise |
| **Fluorescein angiography** (for posterior uveitis) | Optic disc edema, macular edema, vasculitis | If posterior involvement suspected | Not first line for pure anterior uveitis |

**Single finding that confirms:** **Cells in the anterior chamber on slit lamp** — that’s it.

**Single finding that rules out:** **No cells and no flare** = not active anterior uveitis (but beware of very early presentation where cells are few).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) — in Emergency Department:**

1. **Cycloplegic agent** – prevents posterior synechiae, relaxes ciliary muscle → reduces pain and photophobia.  
   - **Cyclopentolate 1%** 1 drop TID for mild cases, **or** **Atropine 1%** 1 drop BD for moderate-severe (longer acting).  
   - **Why:** Synechiae can form within hours. Breaking them early is key. Also gives dramatic symptom relief.

2. **Topical corticosteroid** – suppresses inflammation.  
   - **Prednisolone acetate 1%** 1 drop every 1–2 hours initially for moderate–severe disease.  
   - **Why:** The most potent topical steroid; achieves high intraocular concentration. Decreases cells and flare.  
   - **Important:** *Do not start if corneal dendrites are present* (HSV keratitis). Always check a fluorescein stain first.

3. **Examine IOP** – if high (>30 mmHg), add a topical beta-blocker: **Timolol 0.5%** 1 drop BD.  
   - **Why:** Inflammatory glaucoma can damage the optic nerve if untreated.

4. **Pertinent history** – ask about back pain, joint pain, rashes, oral/genital ulcers, diarrhea, recent trauma, TB exposure, sexual history.

5. **Ophthalmology referral** – always. They will confirm grade, start treatment, and arrange follow-up.

**Ward / outpatient (24–72 hours):**

- Reassess daily. If cells are reducing, taper topical steroids (e.g., from hourly to 2-hourly to QID, then BID over a week).
- If no improvement in 48 hours, consider:
  - **Oral steroids:** **Prednisolone 40 mg PO daily** for 1 week then taper. **Why:** Severe, bilateral, or posterior involvement.
  - **Sub-Tenon’s or periocular steroid** if non-compliant with drops.
- **Add oral NSAID** (e.g., **Naproxen 500 mg PO BD** for pain) – but does not replace steroids.
- If viral cause suspected (HSV/VZV), add **Acyclovir 400 mg PO 5× daily** or **Valacyclovir 1 g PO TID** for 14 days. **Why:** Topical antivirals are weak; oral treats the infection and prevents recurrence.
- If syphilis or TB identified, treat per guidelines (penicillin, anti-TB therapy) **and** continue topical steroids as needed to prevent inflammation worsening (Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction).

**Long-term:**

- **Treat the underlying cause:** 
  - HLA-B27: NSAIDs, sulfasalazine, TNF-alpha inhibitors (e.g., adalimumab) if recurrent.
  - Sarcoid: oral steroids ± methotrexate.
  - JIA: disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs); eye surveillance is critical because flares are silent.
- **Monitor for complications:**
  - IOP checks every visit.
  - Cataract: steroid-induced (treat with IOL only when inflammation quiescent).
  - Macular edema: OCT if vision loss disproportionate to anterior chamber cells.
- **patient education:** recognize redness, pain, photophobia; early self-referral; keep a small bottle of dilating drops at home for recurrent attacks (as prescribed).

**What NOT to do:**

- ❌ **Do not** start topical steroids without examining the cornea — risk of uncontrolled HSV keratitis → corneal ulcer, scarring.
- ❌ **Do not** prescribe topical anesthetics — risk of corneal toxicity and addiction.
- ❌ **Do not** use topical NSAIDs alone (no cycloplegia, no synechiae prevention).
- ❌ **Do not** assume it’s just “conjunctivitis” and give antibiotics only.
- ❌ **Do not** forget to dilate to break synechiae early.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Acute Anterior Uveitis | Acute Conjunctivitis | Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma | Corneal Abrasion / Keratitis |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Dull ache; worse with accommodation and light | Gritty, foreign body sensation | Severe, throbbing, often with headache/nausea | Sharp, worse with blinking |
| **Photophobia** | Marked | Mild to moderate | Moderate (because IOP high) | Severe (especially with epithelial defect) |
| **Discharge** | Watery (tearing) | Watery (viral) or purulent (bacterial) | Watery | Watery |
| **Conjunctival injection** | Ciliary flush (circumcorneal) | Diffuse, more conjunctival | Diffuse + ciliary flush | Diffuse + ciliary flush if deep infiltrate |
| **Pupil** | Small, irregular (synechiae) | Normal, reactive | Mid-dilated, fixed (6–7 mm) | Usually normal, possibly small (if iritis secondary) |
| **IOP** | Normal or mildly elevated (often low) | Normal | Very high (40–60 mmHg) | Normal |
| **Corneal findings** | Fine KP (deposits) on endothelium; clear stroma | Clear, rarely punctate staining | Steamy, edematous; microcystic | Stains with fluorescein (ulcer or defect) |
| **Anterior chamber** | Cells + flare | No cells (rarely occasional cell) | Shallow; no cells/flare (unless absolute glaucoma) | Usually clear (unless secondary uveitis) |
| **Vision** | Blurred (from cells/flare) | Normal or mildly blurred | Severe blurring (cloudy cornea, fixed pupil) | Decreased if over the visual axis |
| **Key differentiator** | Slit lamp: cells + flare | No cells; discharge pattern | IOP > 30, shallow chamber, fixed mid-dilated pupil | Fluorescein staining positive |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Posterior synechiae** | Irregular pupil, blurring | Small, irregular pupil that does not dilate fully; seen on slit lamp | Break early with cycloplegics. If chronic, may require laser iridotomy or surgical synechialysis |
| **Iris bombe → angle-closure glaucoma** | Severe pain, red eye, headache, nausea, cloudy cornea | Very high IOP (>40 mmHg), shallow anterior chamber, iris bulging forward | Urgent ophthalmology: topical + oral IOP-lowering agents, laser peripheral iridotomy to relieve pupillary block |
| **Uveitic glaucoma** (secondary open-angle) | Painless vision loss over months | Elevated IOP, optic disc cupping, nerve fiber layer loss on OCT | Medical management (beta-blockers, alpha-agonists, CAIs). May need trabeculectomy |
| **Cataract** | Gradual vision loss | Lens opacities (posterior subcapsular cataract, often steroid-induced) | Cataract surgery only when inflammation quiet for ≥3 months; perioperative steroids |
| **Cystoid macular edema (CME)** | Central vision loss, metamorphopsia, micropsia | OCT shows cystoid spaces in macula; fluorescein angiography shows petaloid leakage | Topical NSAIDs (ketorolac), periocular or systemic steroids; ± acetazolamide |
| **Band keratopathy** | White, chalky horizontal band across cornea | Subepithelial calcium deposition (often in chronic JIA uveitis) | Treatment: EDTA chelation, excimer laser phototherapeutic keratectomy |

**Late-presentation pattern (what happens if untreated):**  
Patient presents with a blind, painful eye. The pupil is irregular and fixed. Cornea is cloudy. IOP is very high. There is a mature cataract. This is a chronically inflamed eye with glaucoma and cataract — the result of years of undiagnosed or undertreated anterior uveitis. Management is often surgical but prognosis for vision is poor.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **IRITIS: Inflammatory, Reactive, Infectious, Traumatic, Immune, Systemic** (causes) — but more precisely:
  - **I**diopathic
  - **R**eactive arthritis / **R**eiter’s
  - **I**nfections (HSV, VZV, TB, Syphilis)
  - **T**rauma
  - **I**BD (Crohn’s, UC)
  - **S**arcoid / **S**pondyloarthropathy

- **PANDA** for red eye differential:  
  **P**ainful, **A**cute, **N**on-discharging, **D**eep injection, **A**bnormal pupil = *iritis* until proven otherwise.

- **SNOOP** mnemonic for *“should I explore for systemic disease?”*  
  **S**cleritis (or **S**yphilis/TB)  
  **N**odular iritis / **N**euro-ophthalmic (optic nerve)  
  **O**ld (age > 50?) or **O**ral ulcers  
  **O**ther: **O**cular hypotony, **O**rgan involvement (joints, skin, lungs)  
  **P**osterior involvement (vitreous cells, retinitis)

**Clinical pearls (pattern recognition):**

1. **Unilateral red eye + photophobia = iritis until proven otherwise.** Always do a slit lamp.
2. **Ciliary flush is circumcorneal** – the injection is deepest at the limbus and fades toward the fornix. Conjunctivitis is the opposite (more in fornices).
3. **If the pupil is irregular, assume posterior synechiae have formed.** Do not force dilation; use cycloplegics.
4. **Hypopyon + anterior uveitis = Behçet** until proven otherwise. Also consider HLA-B27 and infectious endophthalmitis (especially post-surgery).
5. **In pediatric red eye, always think JIA-associated iritis** — the eye is quiet (no redness, no pain), but you will find cells. Screening is law.
6. **Any patient with uveitis of unknown cause should be asked about back pain** (ankylosing spondylitis). Night pain, morning stiffness, improvement with movement.
7. **Do not start topical steroids without fluorescein staining** — you will miss HSV dendrites and cause a “steroid-modified”

rawMarkdown:
## Anterior Uveitis / Iritis

**One-liner:** Acute inflammation of the iris and ciliary body causing painful red eye, photophobia, and blurred vision; common, treatable, but blind if missed.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of a red, painful left eye. She says the pain is a dull ache that worsens when she moves her eye or looks at bright lights; she keeps the curtains drawn. She reports blurred vision in that eye and a small headache. On exam, her visual acuity is 6/9 (20/30) in the affected eye. There is a ring of redness around the cornea (ciliary flush) and the left pupil is smaller than the right and slightly irregular. She winces when you shine a light (photophobia). You perform a slit lamp exam and see cells and flare in the anterior chamber.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Unilateral red eye | Rules out most bilateral causes (conjunctivitis, dry eye) — suggests intraocular inflammation |
| Pain on eye movement | Ciliary spasm from inflammation; not seen in simple conjunctivitis |
| Photophobia (bright lights → pain) | Miosis and iris spasm; hallmark of anterior uveitis |
| Blurred vision | Inflammatory cells and protein in the aqueous humor scatter light; also possible corneal edema |
| Ciliary flush (circumcorneal injection) | Deep injection around the limbus — indicates pathology in the anterior chamber or cornea |
| Small, irregular pupil | Posterior synechiae: the iris sticks to the lens — a *pathognomonic* sign of iritis |
| Cells and flare on slit lamp | The *sine qua non* — white cells (cells) and protein (flare) in the aqueous humor |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Bilateral, chronic, insidious** – patient may have no pain, just mild blurring. Think *sarcoidosis* or *sympathetic ophthalmia* (rare).
- **Granulomatous iritis** – you see large, “mutton-fat” keratic precipitates on the corneal endothelium and iris nodules. Etiology: sarcoid, TB, syphilis.
- **Pediatric iritis** – child with a white, painless red eye; no photophobia. Often associated with *juvenile idiopathic arthritis* (ANA+). Can be missed until band keratopathy or cataract develops.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Anterior uveitis (iritis) is inflammation of the iris and the ciliary body — the front part of the uveal tract. That inflammation leaks white blood cells and protein into the front of the eye (anterior chamber), causing a red, painful, light-sensitive eye with blurred vision.

**Why it matters:**

- **Frequency:** It is the most common form of uveitis (about 50% of all uveitis cases). You will see it on call.
- **Stakes:** Untreated, it leads to posterior synechiae → iris bombe → acute angle-closure glaucoma (blindness). Also, cataract, chronic glaucoma, and macular edema are possible.
- **Common pitfall:** Mistaking it for simple conjunctivitis and not doing a slit lamp exam. Antibiotic drops won’t help; topical steroids can cure it — if used correctly. Miss the diagnosis, and vision is lost.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

The disease is a story of **disrupted blood–aqueous barrier** and **immune activation**.

1. **Trigger** (infectious, autoimmune, trauma, idiopathic) → activation of Toll-like receptors and release of cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) → vasodilation and increased vascular permeability in the iris and ciliary body.
2. **Protein leaks** from blood into the anterior chamber → **flare** (visible on slit lamp as a beam of light in the aqueous humor).
3. **White blood cells** (neutrophils, lymphocytes, plasma cells) enter the aqueous humor → **cells**.
4. **Prostaglandins** (from COX-2 pathway) cause *ciliary muscle spasm* → **pain on accommodation (reading)** and **deep ache**.
5. **Iris sphincter spasm** (also prostaglandin-mediated) → **miosis**. This is why the pupil is small. The spasm causes **photophobia** (light tries to make the pupil smaller, hitting an already spastic muscle → pain).
6. **Fibrin exudate** forms from the leak → if the iris touches the lens, fibrin can glue them together → **posterior synechiae**. That's the irregular pupil — when you dilate it, the adhesions pull the pupil into a “daisy” shape.
7. If synechiae are 360°, aqueous humor cannot flow from behind the iris into the anterior chamber → **iris bombe** → the iris bulges forward → blocks the trabecular meshwork → **acute angle-closure glaucoma**.

**Link to pattern recognition:**  
- Unilateral → because a local trigger (e.g., HSV reactivation) or a systemic disease (HLA-B27) often affects one eye first.
- Ciliary flush → deep injection from dilated ciliary vessels (not superficial conjunctival vessels).
- Cells/flare → direct evidence of breakdown of blood–aqueous barrier.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | No cause found – 50% of cases | Young adult, no systemic symptoms, negative workup |
| **HLA-B27 related** | Ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis (Reiter’s), psoriatic arthritis, IBD | Unilateral, recurrent; ask about back pain, heel pain, urethritis, diarrhea, skin rash, nail pits. Pain is classic: *“wakes me up at night, better with movement”* |
| **Infectious** | HSV-1 (most common viral cause) | Corneal dendrites on fluorescein; decreased corneal sensation |
| | VZV (shingles) | Previous dermatomal rash; Hutchinson’s sign (vesicles on nose tip – nasociliary nerve) |
| | Toxoplasmosis (congenital or acquired) | Usually posterior uveitis, but can cause anterior in immunocompromised |
| | Syphilis (secondary or tertiary) | Rash, chancre, lymphadenopathy; often bilateral granulomatous |
| | Tuberculosis | Granulomatous; chest X-ray, IGRA positive; exposure history |
| | CMV (in immunocompromised) | Very high IOP, corneal endotheliitis; in HIV with CD4 < 100 |
| **Systemic autoimmune** | Sarcoidosis | Bilateral, granulomatous, mutton-fat KP; CXR shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy; elevated ACE |
| | Behçet disease | Oral ulcers, genital ulcers, skin lesions, arthritis; hypopyon is classic (visible layer of pus in anterior chamber) |
| | Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) | Young girl (5-10 years), ANA-positive, asymptomatic eye – no pain, no redness until late. Screening slit lambs every 3-6 months |
| **Trauma** | Blunt or penetrating injury | History of trauma; high suspicion for infectious endophthalmitis if penetrating |
| **Post-surgical** | After cataract surgery (toxic lens syndrome or pseudophakic uveitis) | Recent eye surgery, onset within days to weeks; can be infectious (endophthalmitis) or sterile inflammation |

**Most common cause overall: Idiopathic (~50%).** In patients with a systemic association, **HLA-B27-related** is the most common identifiable cause.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** None formal — a clinical diagnosis made by **slit lamp exam** showing cells and flare in the anterior chamber.

**Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Slit lamp exam** | Cells (0–4 grade), flare, keratic precipitates, synechiae | Diagnostic; also grades severity | Cells ≥ 1+ = diagnostic. Flare alone can be from low protein (like in quiet eye). |
| **Intraocular pressure (IOP)** | Can be low (due to decreased aqueous secretion) or high (due to trabecular inflammation or synechiae) | Baseline and to monitor for glaucoma | IOP < 10 = may be due to ciliary shutdown; IOP > 21 = suspect inflammatory glaucoma |
| **Dilated fundus exam** | Check for posterior synechiae, vitreous cells, disc edema | To rule out posterior involvement (panuveitis) and to break synechiae early | Dilating preps are essential |
| **Blood tests** (ESR, CRP) | Elevated in systemic inflammation | Screen for underlying disease | Non-specific; done if recurrent, bilateral, or granulomatous |
| **HLA-B27** | Positive in ~50% of acute anterior uveitis | To identify spondyloarthropathy | If positive, ask about back/joint symptoms even if absent on history |
| **Syphilis serology** (VDRL/TPPA) | Positive in syphilis | Mandatory in all uveitis (can mimic anything) | Even if no rash/chancre, treat if positive |
| **Quantiferon-TB (IGRA)** | Positive in latent TB infection | Consider in granulomatous or refractory cases | Start TB treatment if positive (after ruling out active TB) |
| **ANA** | Positive in JIA | In pediatric patients or young women with chronic bilateral disease | If positive, refer to rheumatology |
| **Chest X-ray** | Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (sarcoid), TB, other | Screen for sarcoid and TB | If bilateral hilar adenopathy on CXR and uveitis = sarcoid until proven otherwise |
| **Fluorescein angiography** (for posterior uveitis) | Optic disc edema, macular edema, vasculitis | If posterior involvement suspected | Not first line for pure anterior uveitis |

**Single finding that confirms:** **Cells in the anterior chamber on slit lamp** — that’s it.

**Single finding that rules out:** **No cells and no flare** = not active anterior uveitis (but beware of very early presentation where cells are few).

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) — in Emergency Department:**

1. **Cycloplegic agent** – prevents posterior synechiae, relaxes ciliary muscle → reduces pain and photophobia.  
   - **Cyclopentolate 1%** 1 drop TID for mild cases, **or** **Atropine 1%** 1 drop BD for moderate-severe (longer acting).  
   - **Why:** Synechiae can form within hours. Breaking them early is key. Also gives dramatic symptom relief.

2. **Topical corticosteroid** – suppresses inflammation.  
   - **Prednisolone acetate 1%** 1 drop every 1–2 hours initially for moderate–severe disease.  
   - **Why:** The most potent topical steroid; achieves high intraocular concentration. Decreases cells and flare.  
   - **Important:** *Do not start if corneal dendrites are present* (HSV keratitis). Always check a fluorescein stain first.

3. **Examine IOP** – if high (>30 mmHg), add a topical beta-blocker: **Timolol 0.5%** 1 drop BD.  
   - **Why:** Inflammatory glaucoma can damage the optic nerve if untreated.

4. **Pertinent history** – ask about back pain, joint pain, rashes, oral/genital ulcers, diarrhea, recent trauma, TB exposure, sexual history.

5. **Ophthalmology referral** – always. They will confirm grade, start treatment, and arrange follow-up.

**Ward / outpatient (24–72 hours):**

- Reassess daily. If cells are reducing, taper topical steroids (e.g., from hourly to 2-hourly to QID, then BID over a week).
- If no improvement in 48 hours, consider:
  - **Oral steroids:** **Prednisolone 40 mg PO daily** for 1 week then taper. **Why:** Severe, bilateral, or posterior involvement.
  - **Sub-Tenon’s or periocular steroid** if non-compliant with drops.
- **Add oral NSAID** (e.g., **Naproxen 500 mg PO BD** for pain) – but does not replace steroids.
- If viral cause suspected (HSV/VZV), add **Acyclovir 400 mg PO 5× daily** or **Valacyclovir 1 g PO TID** for 14 days. **Why:** Topical antivirals are weak; oral treats the infection and prevents recurrence.
- If syphilis or TB identified, treat per guidelines (penicillin, anti-TB therapy) **and** continue topical steroids as needed to prevent inflammation worsening (Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction).

**Long-term:**

- **Treat the underlying cause:** 
  - HLA-B27: NSAIDs, sulfasalazine, TNF-alpha inhibitors (e.g., adalimumab) if recurrent.
  - Sarcoid: oral steroids ± methotrexate.
  - JIA: disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs); eye surveillance is critical because flares are silent.
- **Monitor for complications:**
  - IOP checks every visit.
  - Cataract: steroid-induced (treat with IOL only when inflammation quiescent).
  - Macular edema: OCT if vision loss disproportionate to anterior chamber cells.
- **patient education:** recognize redness, pain, photophobia; early self-referral; keep a small bottle of dilating drops at home for recurrent attacks (as prescribed).

**What NOT to do:**

- ❌ **Do not** start topical steroids without examining the cornea — risk of uncontrolled HSV keratitis → corneal ulcer, scarring.
- ❌ **Do not** prescribe topical anesthetics — risk of corneal toxicity and addiction.
- ❌ **Do not** use topical NSAIDs alone (no cycloplegia, no synechiae prevention).
- ❌ **Do not** assume it’s just “conjunctivitis” and give antibiotics only.
- ❌ **Do not** forget to dilate to break synechiae early.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Acute Anterior Uveitis | Acute Conjunctivitis | Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma | Corneal Abrasion / Keratitis |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Dull ache; worse with accommodation and light | Gritty, foreign body sensation | Severe, throbbing, often with headache/nausea | Sharp, worse with blinking |
| **Photophobia** | Marked | Mild to moderate | Moderate (because IOP high) | Severe (especially with epithelial defect) |
| **Discharge** | Watery (tearing) | Watery (viral) or purulent (bacterial) | Watery | Watery |
| **Conjunctival injection** | Ciliary flush (circumcorneal) | Diffuse, more conjunctival | Diffuse + ciliary flush | Diffuse + ciliary flush if deep infiltrate |
| **Pupil** | Small, irregular (synechiae) | Normal, reactive | Mid-dilated, fixed (6–7 mm) | Usually normal, possibly small (if iritis secondary) |
| **IOP** | Normal or mildly elevated (often low) | Normal | Very high (40–60 mmHg) | Normal |
| **Corneal findings** | Fine KP (deposits) on endothelium; clear stroma | Clear, rarely punctate staining | Steamy, edematous; microcystic | Stains with fluorescein (ulcer or defect) |
| **Anterior chamber** | Cells + flare | No cells (rarely occasional cell) | Shallow; no cells/flare (unless absolute glaucoma) | Usually clear (unless secondary uveitis) |
| **Vision** | Blurred (from cells/flare) | Normal or mildly blurred | Severe blurring (cloudy cornea, fixed pupil) | Decreased if over the visual axis |
| **Key differentiator** | Slit lamp: cells + flare | No cells; discharge pattern | IOP > 30, shallow chamber, fixed mid-dilated pupil | Fluorescein staining positive |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Posterior synechiae** | Irregular pupil, blurring | Small, irregular pupil that does not dilate fully; seen on slit lamp | Break early with cycloplegics. If chronic, may require laser iridotomy or surgical synechialysis |
| **Iris bombe → angle-closure glaucoma** | Severe pain, red eye, headache, nausea, cloudy cornea | Very high IOP (>40 mmHg), shallow anterior chamber, iris bulging forward | Urgent ophthalmology: topical + oral IOP-lowering agents, laser peripheral iridotomy to relieve pupillary block |
| **Uveitic glaucoma** (secondary open-angle) | Painless vision loss over months | Elevated IOP, optic disc cupping, nerve fiber layer loss on OCT | Medical management (beta-blockers, alpha-agonists, CAIs). May need trabeculectomy |
| **Cataract** | Gradual vision loss | Lens opacities (posterior subcapsular cataract, often steroid-induced) | Cataract surgery only when inflammation quiet for ≥3 months; perioperative steroids |
| **Cystoid macular edema (CME)** | Central vision loss, metamorphopsia, micropsia | OCT shows cystoid spaces in macula; fluorescein angiography shows petaloid leakage | Topical NSAIDs (ketorolac), periocular or systemic steroids; ± acetazolamide |
| **Band keratopathy** | White, chalky horizontal band across cornea | Subepithelial calcium deposition (often in chronic JIA uveitis) | Treatment: EDTA chelation, excimer laser phototherapeutic keratectomy |

**Late-presentation pattern (what happens if untreated):**  
Patient presents with a blind, painful eye. The pupil is irregular and fixed. Cornea is cloudy. IOP is very high. There is a mature cataract. This is a chronically inflamed eye with glaucoma and cataract — the result of years of undiagnosed or undertreated anterior uveitis. Management is often surgical but prognosis for vision is poor.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **IRITIS: Inflammatory, Reactive, Infectious, Traumatic, Immune, Systemic** (causes) — but more precisely:
  - **I**diopathic
  - **R**eactive arthritis / **R**eiter’s
  - **I**nfections (HSV, VZV, TB, Syphilis)
  - **T**rauma
  - **I**BD (Crohn’s, UC)
  - **S**arcoid / **S**pondyloarthropathy

- **PANDA** for red eye differential:  
  **P**ainful, **A**cute, **N**on-discharging, **D**eep injection, **A**bnormal pupil = *iritis* until proven otherwise.

- **SNOOP** mnemonic for *“should I explore for systemic disease?”*  
  **S**cleritis (or **S**yphilis/TB)  
  **N**odular iritis / **N**euro-ophthalmic (optic nerve)  
  **O**ld (age > 50?) or **O**ral ulcers  
  **O**ther: **O**cular hypotony, **O**rgan involvement (joints, skin, lungs)  
  **P**osterior involvement (vitreous cells, retinitis)

**Clinical pearls (pattern recognition):**

1. **Unilateral red eye + photophobia = iritis until proven otherwise.** Always do a slit lamp.
2. **Ciliary flush is circumcorneal** – the injection is deepest at the limbus and fades toward the fornix. Conjunctivitis is the opposite (more in fornices).
3. **If the pupil is irregular, assume posterior synechiae have formed.** Do not force dilation; use cycloplegics.
4. **Hypopyon + anterior uveitis = Behçet** until proven otherwise. Also consider HLA-B27 and infectious endophthalmitis (especially post-surgery).
5. **In pediatric red eye, always think JIA-associated iritis** — the eye is quiet (no redness, no pain), but you will find cells. Screening is law.
6. **Any patient with uveitis of unknown cause should be asked about back pain** (ankylosing spondylitis). Night pain, morning stiffness, improvement with movement.
7. **Do not start topical steroids without fluorescein staining** — you will miss HSV dendrites and cause a “steroid-modified”
